disease.name: HSV Hepatitis
disease.note: Rare but fulminant hepatitis from HSV-1/2, predominantly in immunocompromised and pregnant women (3rd trimester). High mortality (>50%) if untreated. May lack classic mucocutaneous lesions in 50% of cases - high index of suspicion needed.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Any suspected HSV hepatitis -> EMERGENCY; Proven HSV hepatitis -> ICU; Fulminant liver failure -> TRANSPLANT EVALUATION

emergency.action: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h immediately upon suspicion (do NOT wait for confirmation)
Admit to ICU for monitoring of acute liver failure
Evaluate for liver transplant if fulminant hepatic failure develops
Volume resuscitation and vasopressors for shock

inpatient.action: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for 14-21 days (adjust for renal function)
Monitor LFTs, INR, viral load daily during acute phase
N-acetylcysteine IV for acute liver failure
Transplant listing if King's College criteria met

outpatient.action: Oral valacyclovir 1g PO TID after IV step-down (after clinical improvement)

refractory.action: Liver transplantation for irreversible acute liver failure
Exchange transfusion/plasmapheresis for massive hepatic necrosis

preventive.action: Acyclovir prophylaxis in high-risk pregnant women with recurrent HSV in 3rd trimester
Acyclovir prophylaxis in HSV-seropositive transplant recipients
Cesarean delivery if active genital lesions at time of labor

disease.pathophysiology: HSV directly infects hepatocytes via viral glycoproteins binding to host cell receptors. Lytic viral replication causes widespread hemorrhagic necrosis of liver parenchyma. Massive release of transaminases into circulation (AST/ALT >5000 IU/L). The necrosis is often patchy but confluent, producing characteristic geographic necrotic zones on pathology. Systemic inflammatory response syndrome and DIC from hepatocellular necrosis. Portal hypertension develops from sinusoidal destruction. Without prompt antiviral therapy, rapid progression to multiorgan failure and death.

diagnosticClues.clue: Massive transaminase elevation (AST/ALT >5000 IU/L) disproportionate to bilirubin
Pregnant patient (3rd trimester) or immunocompromised with acute hepatitis
Rapid progression to acute liver failure with encephalopathy
History of genital or oral herpes (present in ~50%)

exclusionClues.clue: Negative HSV PCR from blood and/or liver biopsy
Alternative cause of acute liver failure confirmed (drug, HBV, ischemic)
Transaminases falling rapidly with supportive care alone (suggests ischemic hepatitis)
